questions.question: Explain the difference between singly and doubly linked lists. Also mention one advantage and one disadvantage of each type.
keyPoints: Singly linked: one pointer (next), forward traversal only; Doubly linked: two pointers (next, prev), bidirectional
Advantage singly: less memory, simpler; Advantage doubly: can traverse backwards, efficient deletion at tail
Disadvantage singly: cannot traverse backward, O(n) deletion at end; Disadvantage doubly: extra memory for prev pointer, more complex

questions.question: Describe the steps to insert a new node at the beginning of a singly linked list. Also state the time complexity of this operation.
keyPoints: Create new node using malloc (C) or new (C++/Java)
Set new node's data field
Set new node's next pointer to current head
Update head pointer to point to new node
Time complexity: O(1) - constant time as no traversal needed

questions.question: What are the advantages of linked lists over arrays? Also mention two situations where arrays are preferred over linked lists.
keyPoints: Advantages: dynamic size, efficient insertion/deletion O(1), no memory wastage, can represent complex data
Arrays preferred when: random access needed frequently (O(1) vs O(n)), memory is limited (no pointer overhead), cache performance matters

questions.question: Write an algorithm to delete a node from a singly linked list when the position of the node is given.
keyPoints: Handle edge cases: empty list, deletion at head
If deletion at head: move head to next node, free old head
Otherwise: traverse to node before target position
Update previous node's next to skip target node
Free target node's memory
Time complexity: O(n) for position-based deletion

questions.question: What is a circular linked list? Differentiate between singly circular and doubly circular linked lists.
keyPoints: Circular: last node's next points back to first node (head)
Singly circular: only next pointers form cycle, traversal still one direction
Doubly circular: both next and prev pointers form complete bidirectional cycle
Applications: round-robin scheduling, continuous loops

questions.question: Explain the concept of a header node in linked lists. What are its advantages?
keyPoints: Header node: dummy node at beginning, does not store actual data
Used to simplify implementation and eliminate special cases
Advantages: uniform treatment of operations, no separate code for empty list
Disadvantage: extra memory for one unused node

questions.question: Describe Floyd's cycle detection algorithm. How does it detect a loop in a linked list?
keyPoints: Also called Tortoise and Hare algorithm
Use two pointers: slow (moves 1 step) and fast (moves 2 steps)
If there is a loop, fast will eventually meet slow
Time complexity: O(n), Space complexity: O(1)
Does not modify the list or use extra memory

questions.question: Write a complete C program to implement a singly linked list with operations: (a) Insert at beginning (b) Insert at end (c) Delete from beginning (d) Display the list.
keyPoints: Define struct Node with data and next pointer
Implement createNode function using malloc
Insert at beginning: new->next = head, head = new
Insert at end: traverse to last node, new->next = NULL, last->next = new
Delete from beginning: temp = head, head = head->next, free(temp)
Display: traverse from head, print data until NULL
Include proper header files, main function, and menu

questions.question: Explain the implementation of a stack using linked list. Write pseudocode for push and pop operations and analyze their time complexities.
keyPoints: Stack using linked list: insertion and deletion at head only (LIFO)
Push: create node, new->next = top, top = new - O(1)
Pop: check underflow, temp = top, top = top->next, free(temp) - O(1)
Advantages over array: dynamic size, no overflow (unless memory full)
Comparison with array stack: O(1) both, but linked list uses extra memory for pointers

questions.question: Compare linked lists and arrays as data structures. Provide a detailed analysis including time complexities of various operations and suitable applications for each.
keyPoints: Arrays: contiguous memory, O(1) access, O(n) insertion/deletion
Linked Lists: scattered memory, O(n) access, O(1) insertion/deletion at known position
Memory: arrays fixed, linked lists dynamic
Cache: arrays better due to locality
Applications: arrays for lookup-heavy, linked lists for frequent modifications
Hybrid: dynamic arrays combine benefits with occasional resizing cost

questions.question: Write a complete program to reverse a singly linked list. Explain the algorithm and its time and space complexity.
keyPoints: Three approaches: iterative (using 3 pointers), recursive, using stack
Iterative: prev=NULL, current=head, while current: next=current->next, current->next=prev, prev=current, current=next
After loop, prev is new head
Time: O(n) - single traversal
Space: O(1) - only three pointers used, no extra memory
Recursive: base case empty/single node, recursive call returns reversed rest, adjust pointers
